## PCA compresses variation, not meaning

Principal component analysis (PCA) is one of the main unsupervised tools in `TEM 0052`
because it gives a principled way to replace many correlated variables with a smaller
set of synthetic directions. The key idea is not to keep the original features
intact. The key idea is to keep as much variation as possible while working in a
lower-dimensional coordinate system.

That makes PCA useful for visualization, compression, and preprocessing. It does not
make it a free prediction upgrade.

## Principal components are directions of maximal variance

PCA searches for new axes in the feature space:

1. the first principal component captures the largest possible variance,
2. the second captures the next largest variance subject to being orthogonal to the
   first,
3. later components continue that pattern.

So PCA is not selecting the "best original variables." It is constructing linear
combinations of the original variables.

In the legacy course material, the seeds example is useful because several geometric
measurements move together. PCA turns that correlation structure into a smaller set of
summary directions that are easier to inspect.

## Explained variance is the compression rule

Each component comes with an explained-variance contribution. That is the practical
summary students should inspect before deciding how many components to retain.

- if the first two components explain most of the total variance, a 2D projection may
  be a defensible simplification,
- if the variance is spread more evenly across many components, aggressive
  compression will throw away more information.

This is why PCA is always a trade-off. Fewer dimensions make visualization and
downstream modelling easier, but that convenience comes at the cost of information
loss.

## Feature scaling usually matters before PCA

PCA is variance-driven, so variables on larger numerical scales can dominate the
components even when that scale difference is not substantively important.

That is why standardization is often part of the default workflow:

- without scaling, a large-range predictor can determine the first component mostly
  by units,
- with scaling, PCA focuses more on shared structure than on raw measurement scale.

The wine example from the legacy notebook makes this concrete. PCA on unscaled
features can emphasize the largest-magnitude chemistry measurements, while PCA on
standardized features gives a more balanced summary of the feature set.

::: {.teacher-only}
Ask students whether the first component is "important" because of modelling logic or
because one variable happened to be measured on a larger scale.
:::

## PCA is useful for exploration, not automatic interpretation

PCA helps with three common tasks:

- visualizing high-dimensional data in two dimensions,
- reducing redundancy before a downstream model,
- diagnosing whether a dataset contains a smaller latent structure.

But students should not over-interpret the components. A principal component is a
mathematical direction, not automatically a clean substantive construct. Loadings can
help describe which variables matter most, but the interpretation still requires
judgment.

## Where PCA fits in `TEM 0052`

In this course, PCA belongs in the unsupervised-learning block alongside clustering.
It gives students a first example of a method that is not trying to predict a label.
Instead, it reorganizes the feature space so later inspection or modelling becomes
easier.

That also makes PCA a useful bridge to later workflows:

- exploratory plots before fitting a supervised model,
- dimensionality reduction before classification,
- and a way to discuss what information is lost when we compress data.

## What students should be able to do after this concept

Students should be able to:

- explain what a principal component represents,
- interpret explained-variance ratios as a compression trade-off,
- justify why scaling may matter before PCA,
- distinguish PCA from variable selection,
- and explain when a low-dimensional projection is useful versus misleading.
